payload.text:
You are a senior product manager and full-stack engineer.
Design and (if possible) scaffold a modern integrated web application for the “UPT Pemeliharaan Jalan dan Jembatan (UPT PJJ), Dinas PUPR Kota Banjarmasin”, Indonesia.

Overall goal:
Create an integrated web application where internal stakeholders get an efficient management tool to receive complaints, plan maintenance work, manage stock and equipment, track costs, and generate reports, while the public gets a responsive, transparent, and easy-to-use channel to submit and track road/bridge complaints.

Very important:

Localize all user-facing UI (labels, menus, field names, public pages, etc.) into Bahasa Indonesia.

The domain context is Indonesian local government (Kota Banjarmasin) with the city vision “Banjarmasin Maju Sejahtera”.

Use “JalanKita 2.0” (Indonesia’s road complaint app) as a conceptual benchmark for features and UX, but adapt it to the local UPT PJJ context.

System roles and users:

Public users (warga/masyarakat)

Internal admin/disposer (admin UPT)

Field staff (petugas lapangan)

Warehouse/logistics staff (gudang/material)

Equipment manager (peralatan/armada)

Management/supervisors (pimpinan UPT/Dinas)

High-level requirements:

Localization and general UX

All public and internal UI must be in Bahasa Indonesia (e.g., “Laporan Baru”, “Status Aduan”, “Stok Material”, “Peralatan”, “Jadwal Pekerjaan”, “Biaya Realisasi”, etc.).

Structure the UX so that public users can easily submit complaints with minimal friction, while staff have powerful dashboards and filters.

Design with responsive web in mind and make the architecture easy to extend into a future Android mobile app (e.g., via a shared REST/GraphQL API).

Public complaints portal (Portal Aduan Online)
Build a public-facing web portal with:

Complaint form for the public (Bahasa Indonesia):

Fields: category (e.g., “Jalan Berlubang”, “Jembatan Rusak”, “Drainase Tersumbat”), description, location, optional address text, and upload of photos/videos as evidence.

Use geolocation and EXIF photo metadata to geotag the complaint (if allowed by the user) so the system stores latitude/longitude.

After submitting, the citizen receives a unique ticket/ID for their complaint.

Status tracking page: citizen can enter ticket/ID (and possibly phone/email) to see the current status:

“Diterima”

“Dalam Proses (survey/perbaikan)”

“Selesai”

Notifications: Every status change can trigger a notification (for now model as events that could be sent via SMS/WhatsApp/email in the future).

Display a public map or list of complaints (with anonymized citizen data) showing location, category, and status, so even people who did not submit a report can see which roads/bridges are being handled.

The portal should also include:

“Panduan Melapor” explaining how to submit a proper report and how to choose the correct jurisdiction (national/provincial/city road).

FAQ about the service.

Alternative contacts (e.g., “Call Center 112”, phone numbers of UPT, etc.).

Internal dashboard for complaints and workflow
Build an internal web dashboard for UPT staff with:

A compact overview of all complaints:

List of new reports, current status, category, location, priority, date received.

Admin can filter complaints by status, category, urgency, or geographic area (e.g., kecamatan).

Clear workflow from “aduan masuk” to “pekerjaan selesai”:

Admin can assign each complaint to a specific field team or staff responsible.

Define and store a priority score per complaint (based on rules like severity, road class, traffic impact, etc.). The system should support configurable rules to compute this priority score so resources focus on the most critical complaints.

Once assigned, field staff see their tasks, can update status to: “sedang survei”, “dalam perbaikan”, “selesai diperbaiki”.

Field staff can upload survey results and completion photos.

Each complaint ticket must store detailed action logs:

Survey results (root cause, extent of damage, affected area in m², etc.).

Planned repair: method (e.g., cold mix patching), estimated material quantities, planned start and end date, responsible person.

Actual execution: actual start and finish dates, changes in method, final solution description (e.g., “Ditambal aspal dingin 1 m²”), before/after photos.

The internal dashboard automatically propagates status updates to the public portal (for citizen tracking) and logs all changes end-to-end.

Inventory management: materials and equipment
Design modules for:

a) Material stock (Stok Material)

Maintain a live database of materials: e.g., cold mix asphalt, cement, sand, steel, etc. Each material has:

Current stock quantity (with units).

Minimum threshold.

Warehouse location.

Any time a new job is scheduled or executed, the system can:

Record planned material needs (by supervisor/logistics).

Deduct actual used material from stock when the job is executed.

For example: patching 5 potholes on “Jalan A” requires 50 kg of asphalt; after the job is created and marked as executed, reduce asphalt stock accordingly.

If stock falls below a defined minimum threshold, the system should flag a warning so procurement can be triggered.

Every in/out transaction of material must be logged with date, job reference, quantities, and responsible staff. This transaction history is later used for cost and efficiency analysis.

b) Equipment and fleet management (Manajemen Peralatan)

Maintain a list of equipment and vehicles: e.g., truck patching, stamper/compactor, water tank truck, etc. Each equipment record includes:

Condition (functional, needs maintenance, under repair).

Availability schedule.

Enable scheduling of equipment usage for specific jobs to avoid conflicts.

The system should prevent double-booking the same equipment for overlapping time slots.

Optionally track maintenance schedules for equipment (not necessarily implemented now but design the model to allow future extension).

Cost tracking and efficiency
Implement cost tracking features:

For every completed job, record actual realized cost broken down by:

Materials (based on consumed quantities × unit price).

Transport (e.g., vehicle usage or distance/time).

Labor (e.g., staff hours × rate).

The system should compare realized costs against initial estimates or cost benchmarks (per type of repair or per m²).

Provide analytics to help UPT evaluate efficiency:

Identify over-budget jobs or potential waste.

Identify jobs that are consistently under budget.

Allow aggregation of costs per road segment, per region, per month, etc., to support budgeting and planning.

Scheduling, calendar, and preventive maintenance
Add a scheduling and calendar module:

Show a calendar view (daily/weekly/monthly) of all planned and active jobs.

Each job has scheduled start/end dates, assigned teams, and assigned equipment.

The calendar should help avoid over-scheduling a particular team or equipment at the same time.

Also support scheduling of preventive maintenance tasks (e.g., routine road patrols, periodic inspections) and distinguish them from reactive jobs coming from public complaints (“trouble call”).

Ensure the system helps coordinate preventive maintenance so it does not conflict with urgent reactive jobs.

Prioritization rules for complaints
Design a simple rule-driven engine or at least a clear data model to:

Assign a numeric or categorical priority to each complaint based on:

Severity of damage (e.g., pothole depth/size or if bridge safety is at risk).

Road class (e.g., arterial, collector, local).

Impact on traffic flow and safety.

Admin can adjust the rules or weights over time (future extension), but for now specify a reasonable default scheme.

Use this priority to sort queues, highlight top-priority complaints on dashboards, and guide resource allocation.

Reporting and analytics
The application must generate periodic reports:

Time windows: daily, weekly, monthly (configurable).

Metrics include at least:

Number of complaints received.

Number handled and completed.

Average response time (from received to first action).

Average resolution time (from received to completed).

Material usage per period.

Cost totals and cost per job.

Reports can be in tabular form and in basic visual charts (bar, line, pie, etc.).

Also generate geo-based summaries (e.g., number of complaints per district/kecamatan).

Ensure reports can be exported (e.g., CSV or PDF) so UPT can use them in formal documents.

Transparency and public information
For the public side:

Show a simplified dashboard summarizing:

Number of complaints received vs. resolved.

Map or list of current works in progress.

Ensure personal data of complainants is not exposed, but the public can see which roads are being worked on.

This supports the “Banjarmasin Maju Sejahtera” vision by demonstrating proactive, transparent infrastructure maintenance.

Notifications and workflow
Model a notification system:

Internal notifications for staff: new complaints, assigned tasks, upcoming jobs, low stock alerts, etc.

External notifications for citizens: complaint received (with ticket ID), updates when status moves from “Diterima” to “Dalam Proses” to “Selesai”.

Notifications should be abstracted so they can be later connected to SMS, WhatsApp, or email gateways. For now, design API endpoints or event hooks where these channels could be plugged in.

Architecture and extensibility

Design the system as a modern web app with:

A clean separation between front-end and back-end.

A well-defined API layer that can later be reused by an Android mobile app.

Use a modular architecture so that later it can integrate with:

WhatsApp Business API.

City-level call center or smart city platforms.

SP4N-LAPOR or other national complaint systems.

Ensure authentication and authorization roles are clearly defined (public vs. various internal roles).

Plan for security best practices and data protection (especially citizen contact details and internal operational data).

What I want from you (Gemini):

First, restate the problem in your own words to show understanding.

Then, propose a high-level system architecture (front-end, back-end, database schema outline, and API design), explicitly mentioning how localization to Bahasa Indonesia will be handled.

Next, define detailed functional specifications for each major module listed above.

Finally, if possible, generate initial code scaffolding or example implementations (e.g., data models, example API endpoints, and some front-end UI components with Indonesian labels) to illustrate how this system would be built and how it supports the “Banjarmasin Maju Sejahtera” vision.

**Analyzing Banjarmasin's Vision**

I'm currently immersed in understanding Banjarmasin's vision for road and bridge maintenance. I'm focusing on the context provided, which is the UPT PJJ's role within the Dinas PUPR. I'm drilling down into the specific goals and desired outcomes implied by "Banjarmasin Bersih, Terang, dan Nyaman" to clarify the vision's true meaning. I am focused on uncovering its implications for road maintenance strategies.


**Deconstructing Requirements Further**

I'm now fully immersed in the details of "Banjarmasin Maju Sejahtera". It's clear we're building an integrated web application for road and bridge maintenance. The focus is dual: a public-facing portal for easy complaint submission and tracking, and an internal system for streamlined work order management and cost tracking. Key constraints include full Bahasa Indonesia localization, referencing "JalanKita 2.0", and using a React/Node.js tech stack. We'll be working with mock data for now.  We have identified the core roles: Public, Admin, Field Staff, Logistics, and Supervisors.


**Outlining System Features**

I'm currently outlining the key features of the internal dashboard. We're prioritizing complaint management with a clear workflow, work order generation, and inventory tracking.  Reporting will cover cost analysis and performance metrics like response times. I'm also planning the technical architecture, aiming for React 18, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS on the frontend.


**Clarifying Map Integration**

I'm now revising my plan for the interactive map. Since direct Leaflet or Google Maps integration isn't feasible given our constraints, I'll use a placeholder component for the map visualization. I'm also confirming we can simplify localization, as the application strictly targets an Indonesian audience.


**Mapping Mock Data & Layout**

I've just been fleshing out the mock data with realistic Indonesian locations and materials, like "Jl. Lambung Mangkurat" and "Aspal Cold Mix".  I've sketched a rough outline of the public-facing and admin layouts, ensuring consistency and clarity.  The plan is to use mock data for initial development, with location data being simulated.


**Structuring the UI Components**

I'm presently building out the UI components, focusing on crucial elements for both admin and public users. I've broken it down into key components like the `Sidebar`, `Navbar`, and `StatusBadge`, and pages like `LandingPage`, `PublicReport`, `PublicTracking`, `AdminDashboard`, and `AdminComplaints`. I'm focusing on ensuring clear navigation and visual cues for each user type. I will also incorporate mock inventory tracking to round out the UI implementation.


**Adding Staff Login Access**

I'm currently finalizing the UI and making the demo more accessible. I'm adding a "Staff Login" link to the footer for easy demo access. This will allow quick access to internal features. I'm also addressing the chart requirements, adopting `recharts` for simple and clear visualizations on the dashboard. I've been refining the user interface tone, striving for a balance of bureaucratic yet modern language, and have begun to handle "Camera/Geo" permissions in metadata, even if simulated. I'm focusing on navigation flow, ensuring a seamless user experience.

Here is the complete, integrated web application design and scaffolding for **"SiJalan Banjarmasin"** (Sistem Informasi Jalan & Jembatan Kota Banjarmasin).

### System Architecture Summary

1.  **Frontend**: React 18 (TypeScript) + Tailwind CSS.
    *   **Strategy**: Mobile-first responsive design.
    *   **Localization**: Native Bahasa Indonesia text.
2.  **Routing**: `react-router-dom` (HashRouter) to separate Public Portal (`/`) from Internal Dashboard (`/admin`).
3.  **Data Structure (Simulated)**:
    *   **Complaints (Aduan)**: Tracks status (`Diterima`, `Survei`, `Pengerjaan`, `Selesai`), priority scores, and geo-coordinates.
    *   **Inventory (Gudang)**: Tracks materials (Asphalt, Cement) and thresholds.
    *   **Equipment (Armada)**: Tracks vehicle availability and scheduling.
4.  **Visualization**: `recharts` for analytics and administrative insights.

### Code Implementation

**Integrating Gemini AI**

I'm currently focused on integrating Gemini AI into the "Si-Pejantan" app. Specifically, I'm concentrating on the "Stok Material" and "Laporan & Biaya" sections. My initial focus is on leveraging Gemini's capabilities for data analysis and generating insightful reports from inventory and financial data. I'm exploring how to best utilize its powerful functionalities.


**Enhancing Data Insights**

I'm now incorporating AI-powered insights into both the inventory and reports pages.  I've added "Sparkles" and "AI Insight" buttons to trigger Gemini's analysis. The AI's focused on predicting stock depletion and suggesting procurement based on mock data.  It'll also analyze budget deviations and recommend cost-saving strategies. The `@google/genai` SDK is the current integration method.


**Implementing AI Advisor**

I've been working on integrating AI advisors into both the inventory and reports sections of the app. I'm importing the `GoogleGenAI` module and using my API key with the `gemini-3-flash-preview` model. In the inventory section, I'm analyzing stock data to identify shortages, and in the reports section, I'm analyzing financial data to pinpoint budget risks. I'm focusing on well-formatted Markdown or structured text output.


**Initiating Code Implementation**

I'm starting the coding phase for integrating Gemini's analysis, focusing on inventory and report generation. The goal is to translate the conceptual AI advisor into functional code within the "Si-Pejantan" app.

I will implement **Gemini Intelligence** into the administrative dashboard to transform raw data into actionable insights. 

### Specifications:

1.  **AI Inventory Advisor (`Inventory.tsx`)**:
    *   **Feature**: A smart "AI Stock Advisor" panel that analyzes current material levels.
    *   **Behavior**: It evaluates which materials are below thresholds and uses AI to recommend procurement priorities and quantities based on "simulated" usage patterns.
    *   **Visuals**: A sleek, translucent "AI Insight" card with a pulse animation and a typing effect for AI-generated recommendations.

2.  **AI Fiscal Analyst (`Reports.tsx`)**:
    *   **Feature**: "Gemini Financial Auditor" integration.
    *   **Behavior**: Analyzes budget deviations, detects over-budget risks in the "Buku Komitmen," and suggests specific fiscal optimizations or re-allocations.
    *   **Visuals**: An expandable AI analysis section at the top of the reports page with structured insights (Risks, Opportunities, and Action Plan).

3.  **Fast Processing**:
    *   Using `gemini-2.5-flash-lite-latest` for near-instant insights to maintain a snappy administrative experience.
